And we defined bias and variance and MSE as a way of quantifying that. And we came up at the end with this result that the model risk, which is to say.

The average mean squared error, right? Mean squared error is equal to the irreducible error that is just a noise in our model plus the variance of our estimator, right? Which we saw in the sort of regression cases we can kind of think of as a measure of model complexity, right? More complex models have higher variance because you change a little bit and you know, because the model is more complex, it can fit all the points exactly.
So it kind of varies a lot more.
As the data changes and then there's model bias and we saw simpler models have higher models so there's a trade off.
More complex models have lower bias but higher variance, and simpler models have higher bias but lower variance.

And the higher and lower don't necessarily equal out. You want to find a sweet spot where you get both of these as small as possible so that the total error is as small as possible, right? So this is kind of the idea we learned where we saw cross validation as a way of helping us pick the best model.

Right. So we can't really deal, we can't really do anything about the noise. But sometimes we can increase model complexity, which might give us slightly higher variance, the cost of greatly reduced bias or we could regularize where we might greatly reduce variance at the cost of slightly increased bias.

So today we're going to answer a different side of that same question, which is thinking about the uncertainty, right? So last time was all about, you know, I'm thinking about this parallel universe concept to measure the quality of how good that estimate is. Today we're going to think about the uncertainty in that estimate and that's going to lead us into something called inference, which is going to then lead into how do we reason about our models, right? When you get a, when you use OLS, we get some coefficients. What do they actually mean?
Is. Is that is that meaning useful in the real world at all, and if so, how?

OK, so to start, let's talk about how we actually create parallel universes, right? Last time we spent a bunch of time on this idea of a parallel universe, right? A different sample. But in the real world, we only ever get one, right? Again, here's the warehouse, you know, we go get our sample, here's our sample mean. And so our best guess for the population average is the mean of our sample.
But how certain or uncertain are we about that? Guess you can imagine two different scenarios, right? In scenario one.

I go to the person who's, you know, constructing a new building who wants to know the average height of all 32,000 undergrads. And scenario one, I tell them, yeah, the average height is like 5 foot 8, but it might be as low as 5 seven, it might be as high as 59.
Right. So I have my estimate and I have some range of uncertainty around.
In the second scenario, I go to the person building it and I say the average height is 57, but it could be as low as 5 feet and it could be as high as 6/6.
Well, that's way less useful, right? The increased amount of uncertainty in that second scenario means that our estimate is a lot less useful, even though it's the same estimate 5 foot 8.
Having a higher uncertainty means it's less useful, right? So we want to quantify the uncertainty.

But also we want to be honest about it, right? Because if it is in fact the case that the there is that much uncertainty, then the people making the building better design for that, obviously with the heights, you know, it's kind of silly that's not going to happen. But you know, you might go out and you might build a model that will predict the sales for the next quarter of some product or the number of customers you're going to find, right. And if you go to your boss and you say, yeah, I think there's going to be, you know, our sales are going to be $7.5 million.
There's a big difference between.
It's going to be like between 7.4 and 7.6.
Versus it's going to be between like 5 and 10.

Right. And you may have cases where both of those are the actual answer. And if it is 5 to 10, you have to make very different plans on the business side. So it's important to communicate that uncertainty.
So that's what we're going to do, right? We're basically going to answer the question, Are you sure about that when we get an estimate?

Right. So again, you know, how do we answer that question? Well, again, we can use this idea of parallel universes, right? Because imagine if we had access to all the parallel universes, right? With every single different sample. We could look at the averages from all those samples and we could see how much those vary, right across all the different parallel universes, across all these different possible samples.
What's the average in each one? And then that'll help us quantify our uncertainty.

But of course, we only ever get to see the one, right? If I had money for, you know, more than one sample of size 100, I would have just used it to get a bigger sample, right? It doesn't really, it's not really realistic to have that, you know, all these other samples.
So if we can't actually observe the parallel universes, then how can we say anything about the variability of our sample mean estimator?
So the way we're going to do this is, as you may have guessed, if you remember from data 8, is to use something called the bootstrap.

So a simple example, think of a bag of M&M's. This is an actual real example from a bag of M&M's. So you know we have all these different colours. The proportion of M&M's that are primary colours, red, blue, yellow is 9 out of 18.
So what we can do is we want to know what is that proportion across all bags of M and Ms. So bootstrap tells us if we sample with replacement from our sample.
Right again we take our sample right of size, in this case 18 and we draw 18 samples with replacement.
That gives us a different sample.
Right. So it's sort of like a synthetic version of what we would get in one of those parallel universes.
And in this sample, we happen to have a lot more greens and fewer Reds, yellows and Blues, right? So our proportion of primary colors is 7 out of 18.
A different sample might end up with more, right? In this case, it's 11 out of 18.
So we can keep doing this over and over and over and this idea of drawing samples from our sample.
If our sample is reflective of the population, then those samples of our sample are going to look kind of like samples from the population, right? That's the big idea behind Bootstrap.

So we do this 10,000 different times. We compute the average, you know, the proportion of primary colors in each one. And then we just, you know, use those proportions in each of the the synthetic bags as our way of communicating our uncertainty.
Right. So what we've done here is we've constructed 10,000 parallel synthetic, synthetic parallel universes.

But again, this requires a big, big assumption. It requires the assumption that the distribution of the sample that we have, my original sample of 100 or that original bag of M&M's had better look a lot like the population if for some reason that original sample is not reflective of the population.
Then any resampling I do from it is also going to have the same problems.
So again, we can't simple from the population you know. So we get our best guess of 68.1 inches and then we randomly resample with replacement.
Why do we resample with replacement? Well, if we sample the same number of points without replacement, then we just get the same points every single time.

Right, If I have 5 numbers and I draw five of them without replacement, then I'm always going to get the same 5 numbers. Very boring, right? But if I sample with replacement, then I might sample some points multiple times, I might not sample some points and so on and so on.
So these synthetic best guesses are our way of representing what's going on in the parallel universes.

But the problem is when you do bootstrap, there's a pretty decent chance that especially if you have a large sample, Bill Gates is not going to be in all of your bootstrap resamples, right? And so the ones that he's in are going to be probably pretty reflective of what you're looking for.

And the ones that he's not in just by chance are going to be way, way, way, way, way, way, way lower, right? Because, again, he makes billions of dollars. Everyone else in that neighborhood probably makes a lot of money, but probably nowhere near as much as Bill Gates.
So the true average income is really high.

But any random sample might not contain Bill Gates. And so the bootstrapping procedure is not going to be an accurate conference rule, right? Because we have this one point that our, our data, our estimate heavily depends on, right? It's a property of our data. Our data are skewed.
This means that bootstrap is not a good choice.

I want to talk now about something that Josh introduced way, way back in Lecture 10, literally half the class ago, about this idea of prediction versus inference.
Right. And so this is a slide from Lecture 10 where?
We talked about inference, prediction and causality.

Right. And whenever you go out to do something, you're usually interested in some using data and doing data science, you're usually interested in some combination or subset of these three things, right? Maybe you care about making predictions. Maybe you care about doing inferences. So predictions being, I want to know, you know, can I do a good job of finding AY hat that's as close as possible to the real Y, right? That's prediction just like give me AY hat.
An estimate for something I want to predict that's as close as possible. Don't really care about anything else, just want to make it as close as possible.
Sometimes we care also about inference.

Inference is the process of reasoning about phenomena that happen, right? Maybe answering questions like, well, you know, maybe I want to predict that someone's height based on their parents height, but I want to understand the relationship between parent sites and children's sites. And maybe I also want to understand how that relationship is mediated by other factors, nutrition and so on. I don't just want to predict a child's height, I want to understand what those relationships are.

Sometimes that understanding might just be, you know, correlational. I just want to look at, you know, what are the correlations. But sometimes I might want to go one step further into the causality column, thinking about when does one thing cause another.
Right. Does eating a more nutritious diet cause children to grow taller versus not?
Right. Does do eating different kinds of grains, do eating meat, vegetables, different foods cause children to be taller or not?

So these are three different things we want. And usually in practice, you almost always might have some combination of the two, right? We often want to strike a balance. Usually we don't care about one necessarily. We care about more than one. How much we care about each one depends on the thing we're looking at.

So in this class so far, we've focused primarily on just prediction, right? All the stuff about OLS, we've focused on minimized mean squared error or mean absolute error, right? We really care about making the error as small as possible, right? Getting our Y hats to be as close as possible to the YS. And you know, we saw all the less, we saw regularization as a tool for for all that.
And we spent a lot less time on interpreting.

Those relationships, right? What is the relationship between X&Y? Yeah, sure, it's nice to make predictions, but maybe I also care about understanding the relationship, right? Maybe when I make a prediction about whether, you know, how much money some new product is going to make, maybe I also care about understanding why is it going to make those that much money? Or are there things that I can do to help it make more money or be more successful in the market?
So this difference is kind of the prediction versus inference distinction.

So as an example, prediction, as a couple examples, prediction problems are things like can we predict how well the stock market is going to do, right? Is the stock market going to go up tomorrow or down tomorrow? You know, is someone going to make another speech that thanks to stock market or not?
Is.

This credit card charge fraudulent, right? If I make a charge, you know my credit card company wants to know whenever there's a charge on my card, is this fraud or not? It's fraud. They should flag it, deny it, maybe send me an alert. If not, let it through.
Or facial recognition, right? If there's a picture of someone, can my phone when I, you know, unlock it, can it accurately detect my face?

And funny examples. So there are these websites where that build these models of like, you know, collections of people's faces and these massive surveillance databases and so on. And Josh and I plugged our faces into, into these, you know, you can go and look and see. The ones for me were way less interesting so I stuck with that one.
But inference on the other hand answers the question how and why? How or why?
Do X&Y relate to each other?

So instead of predicting how much is the stock and market going to go up, maybe I want to understand are the relationships between the overall stock market and certain sectors like tech or pharmaceuticals right? Can I use the behavior of those sectors to better understand the relationship of the overall market? Maybe I don't necessarily care whether one causes the other, but I want to have an understanding of the relationship between the two.

Sometimes when we do inference, we do care about causality though, right? Maybe we want to know what is the effect of getting a college degree on life outcomes? Notice that this is a very different question from Can we predict somebody's life outcome, right? Can we predict how much money somebody's going to make over their lives versus understanding? Does getting a college degree 'cause somebody to make more money in their life?
